title: Seed viability towel test
summary: A small germination test for deciding whether old seeds should be planted, traded, saved, or replaced.
content: Seed packets can look valuable while already being weak. A towel test uses a small counted sample to estimate germination. It does not guarantee harvest, purity, disease freedom, or local adaptation, but it prevents wasting a planting window on failed seed.
steps: Place seeds on clean damp material, not soaked.
Label crop, source, date, and number tested.
Count strong sprouts and failures separately.
warnings: Do not eat treated or unknown seeds.
Do not assume sprouting guarantees harvest.
Do not test the whole batch if seed is scarce.
common_mistakes: Letting the towel dry out or rot.
Calling moldy weak sprouts a success.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Seed viability towel test with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Seed viability towel test if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Seed viability towel test and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Seed viability towel test or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Seed viability towel test are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores food safety, dry storage, clean tools, pests, and water use and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Seed viability towel test”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Seed viability towel test as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Seed viability towel test when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Seed viability towel test.
short_term: For the first hours of Seed viability towel test, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Seed viability towel test remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Seed viability towel test fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Seed viability towel test to the real environment: check food safety, dry storage, clean tools, pests, and water use, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
related_topics: Compost runoff and harvest hygiene boundary
